perturbation.original_text: (b) Confidential Information shall not include any information, however designated, that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed Disclosing Party; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party pursuant to the terms of thisAgreement; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiality owed toDisclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Receiving Party; or(v) constitutes Feedback (as defined in Section 5 of this Agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Confidential Information shall not include any information, however designated, that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed Disclosing Party; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party pursuant to the terms of thisAgreement; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiality owed toDisclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Receiving Party;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exclusion of Feedback from Confidential Information in Section 1(b)(v), it creates a contradiction with Section 5. Section 5 explicitly states that Feedback does not create a confidentiality obligation, but now Feedback could be interpreted as Confidential Information under the modified definition, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Suggestions and Feedback The Receiving Party may from time to time provide suggestions, comments or other feedback (“Feedback”) to the Disclosing Party with respect to ConfidentialInformation provided originally by the Disclosing Party. Both parties agree that allFeedback is and shall be given entirely voluntarily. Feedback, even if designated asconfidential by the Disclosing Party, shall not, absent a separate written agreement,create any confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party. Furthermore, except asotherwise provided herein or in a separate subsequent written agreement between theparties, the Receiving Party shall be free to use, disclose, reproduce, license orotherwise distribute, and exploit the Feedback provided to it as it sees fit, entirelywithout obligation or restriction of any kind on account of intellectual property rightsor otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit exclusion of 'Feedback' from the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1(b)(v). This creates a direct conflict with Section 5, which states that 'Feedback... shall not, absent a separate written agreement, create any confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party.' The omission introduces ambiguity about whether Feedback is now considered Confidential Information, directly contradicting the intent and terms of Section 5. This makes the sections incompatible, causing a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (i) gives the undersigned Disclosing Party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure to allow Disclosing Party a reasonable opportunity to seek aprotective order or equivalent, or (ii) obtains written assurance from the applicable judicial or governmental entity that it will afford the Confidential Information the highest level ofprotection afforded under applicable law or regulation. Notwithstandingthe foregoing, the Receiving Party shall not disclose any computer sourcecode that contains Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party inaccordance with a judicial or other governmental order unless it complieswith the requirement set forth in sub-section (i) of this Section 2(b).
perturbation.changed_text: (i) gives the undersigned Disclosing Party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure to allow Disclosing Party a reasonable opportunity to seek aprotective order or equivalent, or (ii) obtains written assurance from the applicable judicial or governmental entity that it will afford the Confidential Information the highest level ofprotection afforded under applicable law or regulation. Notwithstandingthe foregoing, the Receiving Party shall not disclose any computer sourcecode that contains Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party inaccordance with a judicial or other governmental order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'unless it complies with the requirement set forth in sub-section (i) of this Section 2(b)' from the end of Section 2(b), it creates a contradiction. The section now states that source code containing Confidential Information can be disclosed under a judicial order, but the condition of prior notice is removed. This creates uncertainty about whether the prior notice requirement still applies to source code disclosures.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information of Disclosing Party in accordance with a judicial or other governmental order, provided that ReceivingParty either (i) gives the undersigned Disclosing Party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure to allow Disclosing Party a reasonable opportunity to seek aprotective order or equivalent, or (ii) obtains written assurance from the applicable judicial or governmental entity that it will afford the Confidential Information the highest level ofprotection afforded under applicable law or regulation. Notwithstandingthe foregoing, the Receiving Party shall not disclose any computer sourcecode that contains Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party inaccordance with a judicial or other governmental order.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Receiving Party cannot disclose computer source code containing Confidential Information under a judicial order unless they comply with the notice requirement in subsection (i). The changed text removes the condition of complying with subsection (i), now allowing disclosure of source code under a judicial order without prior notice. The contradicted text states that Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information of Disclosing Party in accordance with a judicial order, provided that Receiving Party either (i) gives notice or (ii) obtains written assurance. The changed text directly contradicts this provision regarding source code, as it removes the notice requirement for source code disclosure under a judicial order. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information orany other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees andconsultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way tohelp Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information andprevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information orany other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees andconsultants.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the cooperation clause from Section 3(a) creates an in-text contradiction, since in Section 2 (d) it mentions that the Receiving party will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure. Thus, creating an uncertainty of what the receiveng party's oblgations are.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (d) Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information orany other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees andconsultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way tohelp Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information andprevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the receiving party will notify the disclosing party and cooperate with them to regain possession of confidential information. The changed text removes the obligation to cooperate. The contradicted text states that the receiving party 'will cooperate'. Thus, the changed text creates a direct contradiction because the receiving party no longer has the explicit obligation to cooperate as mentioned in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Disclosing Party provides any computer software and/or hardware to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information under the terms ofthis Agreement, such computer software and/or hardware may only be used bythe Receiving Party for evaluation and providing Feedback (as defined inSection 5 of this Agreement) to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Disclosing Party provides any computer software and/or hardware to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information under the terms ofthis Agreement, such computer software and/or hardware may only be used bythe Receiving Party for evaluation to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit permission to provide Feedback (and the reference to Section 5) in Section 4(b) creates a contradiction because Section 5 exists to define the terms around Feedback. Now, if software/hardware is provided, the Receiving party does not have implicit authorization to provide feedback.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Suggestions and Feedback The Receiving Party may from time to time provide suggestions, comments or other feedback (“Feedback”) to the Disclosing Party with respect to ConfidentialInformation provided originally by the Disclosing Party. Both parties agree that allFeedback is and shall be given entirely voluntarily. Feedback, even if designated asconfidential by the Disclosing Party, shall not, absent a separate written agreement,create any confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party. Furthermore, except asotherwise provided herein or in a separate subsequent written agreement between theparties, the Receiving Party shall be free to use, disclose, reproduce, license orotherwise distribute, and exploit the Feedback provided to it as it sees fit, entirelywithout obligation or restriction of any kind on account of intellectual property rightsor otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allows the Receiving Party to use provided software/hardware for both evaluation and providing feedback, referencing Section 5. The changed text removes the explicit permission to provide Feedback and the reference to Section 5. Section 5 defines the terms around providing feedback. By removing the authorization to provide feedback in the changed text, it contradicts Section 5, which assumes that the Receiving Party may provide feedback, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.
